question: How many provinces of Afghanistan are there including modern provices like Loya Paktia ?

prediction.answer: Afghanistan has 34 official provinces, including the regions grouped under names like Loya Paktia, which are not separate administrative provinces.
hop1_docs: Loya Paktia | Loya Paktia (Pashto: لويه پکتيا‎ , lit.  "Great Paktia") is a historical and cultural region of Afghanistan, comprising the modern Afghan provinces of Paktia, Paktika, Khost, and also parts of Logar and Ghazni.  The Kurram Agency in Pakistan may be included too.  Loya Paktia is vaguely defined by a common culture and history that is connected to the local indigenous tribes that reside in the region.  Particular styles of clothing, articles of clothing, turban styles, turban cloth colors, dialects of Pashto language, etc. may sometimes be associated with specific tribes indigenous to Loya Paktia and thus integrate themselves into regional culture.  For instance, a Pashtun tribesman from Loy Kandahar may quickly recognize a Pashtun from Loya Paktia based upon his turban (or lungee) style and color.  Likewise, a Pashtun from Loya Paktia may recognize someone from Loy Kandahar based upon his unique style of collarless kameez (shirt) with specific embroidered patterns on the front.  There are many subtle and intricate cultural indicators of this type that are not recorded in any known written history but simply known and observed by the tribesmen of the various Pashtun regions of Afghanistan and Pakistan.

Loy Kandahar | Loy Kandahar (Pashto: لوي کندهار‎ , lit.  "Great Kandahar") is a historical and cultural region of Afghanistan, comprising the modern Afghan provinces of Kandahar, Helmand, Urozgan, Farah, Zabul and Quetta.  Loy Kandahar is vaguely defined by a common culture and history that is connected to the local indigenous tribes that reside in the region.  Some people may refer to these areas as being under the "Kandahari cultural sphere of influence".  Particular styles of clothing, articles of clothing, turban styles, turban cloth colors, dialects of Pashto language, etc. may sometimes be associated with specific tribes indigenous to Loy Kandahar and thus integrate themselves into regional culture.  For instance, a Pashtun tribesman from Loy Kandahar may quickly recognize a Pashtun from Loya Paktia based upon his turban (or lungee) style and color.  Likewise, a Pashtun from Loya Paktia may recognize someone from Loy Kandahar based upon his unique style of collarless kameez (shirt) with specific embroidered patterns on the front.  There are many subtle and intricate cultural indicators of this type that are not recorded in any known written history but simply known and observed by the tribesmen of the various Pashtun regions of Afghanistan and Pakistan.

Zadran (Pashtun tribe) | The Zadran (Pashto: ځدراڼ‎ "dzadrāṇ"; pronounced "dzādroṇ" in Khost-Paktia dialect), also spelled Dzadran, Jadran and Jandran, is a Pashtun tribe that inhabits an area in eastern Afghanistan and parts of Waziristan in neighboring Pakistan.  They are mainly found in parts of the Loya Paktia and Khost areas, ""Zadran: Pashtun tribe mainly residing in the “Zadran Arc” a 9-district area encompassing portions of the khost Paktya, and Paktika provinces."" ,
Paktia University | Paktia University (Pashto: د پکتيا پوهنتون‎ ) is a public institution in the city of Gardez in Paktia Province, eastern Afghanistan.
List of governors of Paktia | This is a list of the governors of the province of Paktia, Afghanistan.

Pomegranate production in Afghanistan | Pomegranate production in Afghanistan is a significant contributor to the Afghan agricultural economy.  Pomegranates are a major fruit crop in many provinces such as Kandahar, Helmand, Wardak, Ghazni, Paktia, Farah, Kapisa and Balkh, and are the source of the livelihoods of thousands of people.

Dawlatzai | Dawlatzai (Pashto: دولتزی‎ ) is a Pashtun sub-tribe of the Tanoli tribe mainly found in eastern Afghanistan.  There are many different and distinct tribes that have this name.  Dawlatzis live in Logar, Samangan, Paktia, Paktika, Khost, Baghlan, Nangarhar, Balkh Tajurghan, and Faryab provinces of Afghanistan.
hop2_docs: Loya Paktia | Loya Paktia (Pashto: لويه پکتيا‎ , lit.  "Great Paktia") is a historical and cultural region of Afghanistan, comprising the modern Afghan provinces of Paktia, Paktika, Khost, and also parts of Logar and Ghazni.  The Kurram Agency in Pakistan may be included too.  Loya Paktia is vaguely defined by a common culture and history that is connected to the local indigenous tribes that reside in the region.  Particular styles of clothing, articles of clothing, turban styles, turban cloth colors, dialects of Pashto language, etc. may sometimes be associated with specific tribes indigenous to Loya Paktia and thus integrate themselves into regional culture.  For instance, a Pashtun tribesman from Loy Kandahar may quickly recognize a Pashtun from Loya Paktia based upon his turban (or lungee) style and color.  Likewise, a Pashtun from Loya Paktia may recognize someone from Loy Kandahar based upon his unique style of collarless kameez (shirt) with specific embroidered patterns on the front.  There are many subtle and intricate cultural indicators of this type that are not recorded in any known written history but simply known and observed by the tribesmen of the various Pashtun regions of Afghanistan and Pakistan.

Zadran (Pashtun tribe) | The Zadran (Pashto: ځدراڼ‎ "dzadrāṇ"; pronounced "dzādroṇ" in Khost-Paktia dialect), also spelled Dzadran, Jadran and Jandran, is a Pashtun tribe that inhabits an area in eastern Afghanistan and parts of Waziristan in neighboring Pakistan.  They are mainly found in parts of the Loya Paktia and Khost areas, ""Zadran: Pashtun tribe mainly residing in the “Zadran Arc” a 9-district area encompassing portions of the khost Paktya, and Paktika provinces."" ,

Paktia Province | Paktia (Pashto: پکتيا‎ – "Paktyā", Persian: ولایت پکتیا‎ ‎ ) is one of the 34 provinces of Afghanistan, located in the east of the country.  The province is divided into eleven districts and has a population of roughly 525,000, which is mostly a tribal society living in rural areas.  Pashtun tribes make up the majority of the population but smaller number of Tajiks are also found.  Gardēz is the provincial capital.

Loy Kandahar | Loy Kandahar (Pashto: لوي کندهار‎ , lit.  "Great Kandahar") is a historical and cultural region of Afghanistan, comprising the modern Afghan provinces of Kandahar, Helmand, Urozgan, Farah, Zabul and Quetta.  Loy Kandahar is vaguely defined by a common culture and history that is connected to the local indigenous tribes that reside in the region.  Some people may refer to these areas as being under the "Kandahari cultural sphere of influence".  Particular styles of clothing, articles of clothing, turban styles, turban cloth colors, dialects of Pashto language, etc. may sometimes be associated with specific tribes indigenous to Loy Kandahar and thus integrate themselves into regional culture.  For instance, a Pashtun tribesman from Loy Kandahar may quickly recognize a Pashtun from Loya Paktia based upon his turban (or lungee) style and color.  Likewise, a Pashtun from Loya Paktia may recognize someone from Loy Kandahar based upon his unique style of collarless kameez (shirt) with specific embroidered patterns on the front.  There are many subtle and intricate cultural indicators of this type that are not recorded in any known written history but simply known and observed by the tribesmen of the various Pashtun regions of Afghanistan and Pakistan.

Khost Province | Khost (Pashto: خوست‎ , Persian: خوست‎ ‎ ) is one of the 34 provinces of Afghanistan, located in the eastern part of the country next to Pakistan's Federally Administered Tribal Areas (FATA).  Khost province used to be part of Paktia province in the past.  The city of Khost serves as the capital of Khost province.  The population of the province is around 546,800, which is mostly a tribal society.  Khost Airport serves the province for domestic flights to Afghanistan's capital, Kabul.
Southern Province, Afghanistan | Southern Province is a defunct province of Afghanistan which was dissolved in 1964 to create the province of Paktia.  The former province's capital was Gardez.

Assadullah Wafa | Assadullah Wafa (born Kandahar) is the former governor of the Afghan province of Helmand, and formerly governor of Kunar Province of Afghanistan and a previous governor of the Paktia Province where he was succeeded by Hakim Taniwal.  His first name is sometimes spelled "Asadullah".  He was born in Kandahar, Afghanistan.

Pomegranates are a major fruit crop in many provinces such as Kandahar, Helmand, Wardak, Ghazni, Paktia, Farah, Kapisa and Balkh, and are the source of the livelihoods of thousands of people.", "Dawlatzai | Dawlatzai (Pashto: \u062f\u0648\u0644\u062a\u0632\u06cc\u200e ) is a Pashtun sub-tribe of the Tanoli tribe mainly found in eastern Afghanistan.  There are many different and distinct tribes that have this name.  Dawlatzis live in Logar, Samangan, Paktia, Paktika, Khost, Baghlan, Nangarhar, Balkh Tajurghan, and Faryab provinces of Afghanistan."]

Reasoning: The question concerns the total number of provinces in Afghanistan, including regions classified under areas like Loya Paktia. From the passages, historical regions like Loya Paktia include provinces such as Paktia, Paktika, Khost, and parts of Logar and Ghazni. However, these references are to cultural and historical regions, not administrative divisions. 

The official number of provinces in Afghanistan is fixed and does not change based on cultural or historical region classifications like Loya Paktia. Afghanistan is composed of 34 provinces as its established administrative divisions. These provinces include Paktia, Paktika, Khost, Logar, and Ghazni, but the categorization under broader regions like Loya Paktia does not alter the administrative count.
Summary: Afghanistan officially has 34 provinces. The mention of regions like Loya Paktia refers to cultural, historical groupings rather than additional administrative divisions.
